metadata.title: Polarization with radio interferometers
metadata.abstract: where 𝐴 and 𝐴 𝑋 𝑌 are the Cartesian amplitude components of the E-field, and 𝛿 𝐴 and 𝐴 𝑋𝑌 is the phase lag between them, and 𝑅 𝐿 are the opposite 𝛿 circular amplitude components of the E-field, and 𝑅𝐿 the phase lag between them.

evidence.original_text: Polarimetry provides - B-field direction - Turbulence - Indirectly: B-field strength. Polarized Radio Emission - Zeeman splitting - Process - Generates polarized emission - Only in spectral lines - If magnetic moment: e.g. HI, OH, CN, H2O - B-field splits RCP and LCP - Separation: 2.8 Hz/mG Polarimetry provides (if detectable) B-field strength at source. Polarized Radio Emission - Scattering/reflection - Unpolarized EM wave scattered by particles; the scattered wave is partially or completely polarized. - Modifies polarization state - Thomson scattering: no T dependence - Planets / Moon: dielectric transition Polarimetry provides: - Electron densities in cool gas - Dust properties - Lunar dielectric constant. Faraday rotation - Process - Modifies polarization state - Delay between LCP and RCP - Rotates linear pol. Angle - Δ𝜒= 𝜒0 + 𝜙𝜆^2 here 𝑛 𝐵∙𝑑𝑙^Ԧ 𝑒 𝜙= 0.812 න there Polarimetry provides - Source plasma properties - Intervening plasma properties - Rare cases: 3D tomography.

We utilize in radio astronomy the parameters defined by George Stokes (1852; 𝐴𝐵𝐶𝐷 ), and introduced to astronomy by Chandrasekhar 𝐼 𝐼 𝑈𝑉 - (1946; 𝑙 𝑟 ): Units of power: Jy, or Jy/beam where 𝐴 and 𝐴 𝑋 𝑌 are the Cartesian amplitude components of the E-field, and 𝛿 𝐴 and 𝐴 𝑋𝑌 is the phase lag between them, and 𝑅 𝐿 are the opposite 𝛿 circular amplitude components of the E-field, and 𝑅𝐿 the phase lag between them. - Monochromatic radiation is 100% polarized: 𝐼 = 𝐴𝑋^2 + 𝐴𝑌^2 = 𝑅^2 + 𝐿^2 𝑄 = 𝐴𝑋^2 − 𝐴𝑌^2 = 2𝑅𝐿 cos𝛿𝑅𝐿 𝑈 = 2𝐴𝑋𝐴𝑌 cos𝛿𝑋𝑌 = 2𝑅𝐿 sin𝛿𝑅𝐿 𝑉 = 𝑅^2 − 𝐿^2. Stokes Parameters – quasi-monochromatic - Monochromatic radiation does not exist • Narrow slices of frequency width Δ𝜐 , for which fields are described by a 1 single amplitude and phase for a period of t~ Δ𝜐. • Averaging time 𝜏≫Δ𝜐^{-1} - Note in this case: (100% polarization is not possible) 𝐼 = ⟨𝐴𝑋^2⟩ + ⟨𝐴𝑌^2⟩ = ⟨𝑅^2⟩ + ⟨𝐿^2⟩ 𝑄 = ⟨𝐴𝑋^2⟩ − ⟨𝐴𝑌^2⟩ = ⟨2𝑅𝐿 cos𝛿𝑅𝐿⟩ 𝑈 = ⟨2𝐴𝑋𝐴𝑌 cos𝛿𝑋𝑌⟩ = ⟨2𝑅𝐿 sin𝛿𝑅𝐿⟩ 𝑉 = ⟨𝑅^2⟩ − ⟨𝐿^2⟩ • Fractional pol.: linear 𝑝= √(𝑄^2 + 𝑈^2) /𝐼≤1 ; circular: 𝑣= 𝑉/𝐼≤1.

Polarimetry with Interferometers - Stokes Visibilities - Interferometer Response to Polarized Emission. Stokes Visibilities • Combining the complex fields at antennas1 and 2, the Stokes visibilities can be written as ℐ = ⟨𝑅1𝑅2*⟩ + ⟨𝐿1𝐿2*⟩ 𝒬 = ⟨𝑅1𝐿2*⟩ + ⟨𝐿1𝑅2*⟩ 𝒰 = 𝑖⟨𝑅1𝐿2*⟩ − 𝑖⟨𝐿1𝑅2*⟩ 𝒱 = ⟨𝑅1𝑅2*⟩ − ⟨𝐿1𝐿2*⟩ • The script symbols ℐ, 𝒬, 𝒰, 𝒱 remind us that these Stokes Visibilities are complex numbers, related to the (real) source brightness through Fourier transform, e.g.: ℐ(𝑢, 𝑣)= ׬ 𝐼𝑒^{+2𝜋𝑖𝜐(𝑢𝑙+𝑣𝑚)/𝑐} d𝑙d𝑚 . 𝑙𝑚 • ℐ ↔ I, 𝒬 ↔ Q, 𝒰 ↔ U, 𝒱 ↔ V. Relation to Sensors - The above is formulated in terms of electric fields measured at two locations. - What is the relation to real sensors (antennas)? - Antennas are polarized – they provide two simultaneous voltage signals whose values are (ideally) representations of the two electric field components – either in circular or linear basis. Our Generic Sensor - Two antennas, each with two differently polarized outputs, produce four complex correlations. • From these four outputs, we want to generate the four complex Stokes’ visibilities ℐ, 𝒬, 𝒰, 𝒱.

Jones Matrix Algebra - From now assume all systems linear: 𝒆𝑖′ = 𝑱𝑖 𝒆𝑖 - 𝑱𝑖 (2x2) is called Jones matrix • Cross correlation: 𝑬𝑖𝑗′ = 𝒆𝑖′ 𝒆𝑗′† = 𝑱𝑖 𝒆𝑖 𝒆𝑗† 𝑱𝑗† = 𝑱𝑖𝑬𝑖𝑗𝑱𝑗† - This is the measurement equation. • Invertible! 𝑬𝑖𝑗′ = 𝑱𝑖𝑬𝑖𝑗𝑱𝑗†. Example Jones Matrices - Perfect instrument: 𝑱𝑖 = [1 0; 0 1] - Polarization leakage: 𝑱𝑖 = [1 𝐷𝑋𝑖; 𝐷𝑌𝑖 1] - Time delay: 𝑱𝑖 = [𝑒^{𝑖𝜔𝜏𝑋𝑖} 0; 0 𝑒^{𝑖𝜔𝜏𝑌𝑖}] - Parallactic angle or feed rotation XY: 𝑱𝑖 = [cos𝜓𝑃 sin𝜓𝑃; -sin𝜓𝑃 cos𝜓𝑃] - Receiver gain: 𝑱𝑖 = [𝐺𝑋𝑖 0; 0 𝐺𝑌𝑖] • Parallactic angle or feed rotation RL: 𝑱𝑖 = [𝑒^{𝑖𝜓𝑃} 0; 0 𝑒^{-𝑖𝜓𝑃}] Each component of the overall system, including propagation effects, can be represented by a Jones matrix and multiplied to obtain a ‘system Jones’ matrix.

Polarized Radio Emission - Synchrotron Emission - Generates polarized emission - Main emission mechanism at cm-m wavelength - Up to 80% linearly polarized (no circular pol.) - ⟨𝐸source⟩⊥ 𝐵source Image Credit: Gemini Observatory Polarimetry provides - B-field direction - Turbulence - Indirectly: B-field strength.

Faraday rotation - Process - Modifies polarization state - Delay between LCP and RCP - Rotates linear pol. Angle - Δ𝜒= 𝜒0 + 𝜙𝜆^2 here 𝑛 𝐵∙𝑑𝑙^Ԧ 𝑒 𝜙= 0.812 න there Polarimetry provides - Source plasma properties - Intervening plasma properties Graphs of polarization angle against wavelength squared for polarized extragalactic sources in the field (Haverkorn, Katgert, & de Bruyn 2003). - Rare cases: 3D tomography.

Polarized Radio Emission - Zeeman splitting - Process - Generates polarized emission - Only in spectral lines - If magnetic moment: e.g. HI, OH, CN, H2O - B-field splits RCP and LCP - Separation: 2.8 Hz/mG Polarimetry provides (if detectable) B-field strength at source. Zeeman splitting –Vlemmings, Diamond, & van Lengevelde (2001) Total Power The brightest H2O maser feature around S Per. The dashed line is the fit of the synthetic circularly polarized spectrum to the observed spectrum. Circular Pol. Also shown are the observed (dashed) and expected (solid) positions of the minimum and maximum of the circular polarization spectrum.

Theory meets real-world - Recovering the Stokes Visibilities - Polarization Calibration of Real Interferometers. Calibration Troubles … - To understand this last point, note that for the linear system: V_Y1Y2 = G_Y1 G_Y2 (ℐ + 𝒬 cos 2𝜓𝑃 + 𝒰 sin 2𝜓𝑃) / 2 V_X1X2 = G_X1 G_X2 (ℐ − 𝒬 cos 2𝜓𝑃 − 𝒰 sin 2𝜓𝑃) / 2 - To calibrate means to solve for 𝐺𝑌 and 𝐺𝑋 terms. - To do so requires knowledge of both Q and U. - Virtually all calibrators have notable, and variable, linear pol. - Meanwhile, for circular: V_R1R2 = G_R1 G_R2 (ℐ + 𝒱) / 2 V_L1L2 = G_L1 G_L2 (ℐ − 𝒱) / 2 - In this case we have no sensitivity to Q or U (good!). Instead, we have a sensitivity to V. - But as it turns out –V is nearly always negligible for the 1000-odd sources that we use as standard calibrators.

Polarized Radio Emission - Synchrotron Emission - Generates polarized emission - Main emission mechanism at cm-m wavelength - Up to 80% linearly polarized (no circular pol.) - ⟨𝐸source⟩⊥ 𝐵source Image Credit: Gemini Observatory Polarimetry provides - B-field direction - Turbulence - Indirectly: B-field strength.

Polarized Radio Emission - Zeeman splitting - Process - Generates polarized emission - Only in spectral lines - If magnetic moment: e.g. HI, OH, CN, H2O - B-field splits RCP and LCP - Separation: 2.8 Hz/mG Polarimetry provides (if detectable) B-field strength at source.

Faraday rotation - Process - Modifies polarization state - Delay between LCP and RCP - Rotates linear pol. Angle - Δ𝜒= 𝜒0 + 𝜙𝜆^2 here 𝑛 𝐵∙𝑑𝑙^Ԧ 𝑒 𝜙= 0.812 න there Polarimetry provides - Source plasma properties - Intervening plasma properties Graphs of polarization angle against wavelength squared for polarized extragalactic sources in the field (Haverkorn, Katgert, & de Bruyn 2003). - Rare cases: 3D tomography.

Stokes Visibilities • Combining the complex fields at antennas1 and 2, the Stokes visibilities can be written as ℐ = ⟨𝑅1𝑅2*⟩ + ⟨𝐿1𝐿2*⟩ 𝒬 = ⟨𝑅1𝐿2*⟩ + ⟨𝐿1𝑅2*⟩ 𝒰 = 𝑖⟨𝑅1𝐿2*⟩ − 𝑖⟨𝐿1𝑅2*⟩ 𝒱 = ⟨𝑅1𝑅2*⟩ − ⟨𝐿1𝐿2*⟩ • The script symbols ℐ, 𝒬, 𝒰, 𝒱 remind us that these Stokes Visibilities are complex numbers, related to the (real) source brightness through Fourier transform, e.g.: ℐ(𝑢, 𝑣)= ׬ 𝐼𝑒^{+2𝜋𝑖𝜐(𝑢𝑙+𝑣𝑚)/𝑐} d𝑙d𝑚 . 𝑙𝑚 • ℐ ↔ I, 𝒬 ↔ Q, 𝒰 ↔ U, 𝒱 ↔ V. Jones Matrix Algebra - From now assume all systems linear: 𝒆𝑖′ = 𝑱𝑖 𝒆𝑖 - 𝑱𝑖 (2x2) is called Jones matrix • Cross correlation: 𝑬𝑖𝑗′ = 𝒆𝑖′ 𝒆𝑗′† = 𝑱𝑖 𝒆𝑖 𝒆𝑗† 𝑱𝑗† = 𝑱𝑖𝑬𝑖𝑗𝑱𝑗† - This is the measurement equation. • Invertible! 𝑬𝑖𝑗′ = 𝑱𝑖𝑬𝑖𝑗𝑱𝑗†. Example Jones Matrices - Perfect instrument: 𝑱𝑖 = [1 0; 0 1] - Polarization leakage: 𝑱𝑖 = [1 𝐷𝑋𝑖; 𝐷𝑌𝑖 1] - Time delay: 𝑱𝑖 = [𝑒^{𝑖𝜔𝜏𝑋𝑖} 0; 0 𝑒^{𝑖𝜔𝜏𝑌𝑖}] - Parallactic angle or feed rotation XY: 𝑱𝑖 = [cos𝜓𝑃 sin𝜓𝑃; -sin𝜓𝑃 cos𝜓𝑃] - Receiver gain: 𝑱𝑖 = [𝐺𝑋𝑖 0; 0 𝐺𝑌𝑖] • Parallactic angle or feed rotation RL: 𝑱𝑖 = [𝑒^{𝑖𝜓𝑃} 0; 0 𝑒^{-𝑖𝜓𝑃}] Each component of the overall system, including propagation effects, can be represented by a Jones matrix and multiplied to obtain a ‘system Jones’ matrix. evidence.section: I and Q Visibilities for Mars at 23 GHz
evidence.original_text: I and Q Visibilities for Mars at 23 GHz Amplitude |I| is close to a 𝐽0 Bessel function. Zero crossing at 20 k 𝜆 tells that Mars is diameter ~10”. |Q| amplitude ~0 at zero baseline 𝜆 means |Q| zero at 30 k pol. Structures ~8“ scale. VLA, 23 GHz, ‘D’ config. (Jan. 2006) Phase I phase alternates between 0 & 𝜋 Q phase = both 0 and 𝜋 in the ‘main lobe’ – this tells us there are both positive and negative structures, at different PA. Mars emits in the radio as a black body. Shown are false-color coded I,Q,U,P images. V is not shown – all noise – no circular polarization. Resolution is 3.5”, Mars’ diameter is ~10” From the Q and U images alone, we can deduce the polarization is radial, around the limb.
VLA Moon – linear system Dirty Stokes I Image of the Moon at ~350 MHz Polarization of the Moon

Pulsars Polarization Position Angle Total intensity Linear Pol. Circular Pol. LWA1; Dike, Taylor, Stovall (2017) Pulsars are highly magnetized => strong polarized emission Study of which provides insights into the poorly understood emission mechanism of Pulsars.
Polarization from Quasar jet Credit: Jorstad/Marscher/NASA/DOE/Fermi LAT Collaboration

evidence.original_text: Jones Matrix Algebra From now assume all systems linear: ′ 𝒆 𝒆 𝑖 = 𝑱𝑖 𝑖 𝑱𝑖 (2x2) is called **Jones matrix** ′ ′ ′† Cross correlation: 𝑬 𝒆 𝒆 𝑖𝑗 𝑖 𝑗 † = 𝒆 𝒆 𝑱𝑖 𝑖 𝑱𝑗 𝑗 † † = 𝒆 𝒆 𝑱𝑖 𝑖 𝑗 𝑱𝑗 † = 𝑱𝑖𝑬𝑖𝑗𝑱𝑗 This is the measurement equation. Invertible! ′ −1 ′ †−1 𝑬𝑖𝑗 = 𝑱𝑖 𝑬𝑖𝑗𝑱𝑗 Example Jones Matrices Perfect instrument: Polarization leakage: Time delay: Parallactic angle or feed rotation XY: Receiver gain: Parallactic angle or feed rotation RL: Each component of the overall system, including propagation effects, can be represented by a Jones matrix and multiplied to obtain a ‘system Jones’ matrix.

entries.description: Standardized polarization calibration using Jones matrix algebra

evidence.original_text: Measuring Cross-Polarization Terms Correction of the X/Y or R/L response for the ‘leakage’ is important, since the D-term amplitude is comparable to the fractional polarization. There are two standard ways to proceed (circular base): 1. Observe a calibrator source of known polarization (preferably zero!) 2. Observe a calibrator of unknown polarization over an extended period. Case 1: Calibrator source known to have zero polarization Single observation should suffice to measure leakage terms. Note: In this approximation, only 2Nant-1 terms can be determined. One must be assumed (usually = 0). All the others are referred to this, thus ‘relative’ D terms. Case 2: Calibrator with significant (or unknown) polarization. You can determine both the (relative) D terms and the calibrator polarizations for an alt-az antenna by observing over a wide range of parallactic angle. (Conway and Kronberg first used this method.) As time passes 𝜓𝑝 changes in a known way. The source polarization term then rotates w.r.t. the antenna leakage term, allowing a separation.

Summary Polarimetry is a little complicated, but do not be afraid! The polarized state of EM radiation gives valuable insights into the physics of the emission. Well designed systems are stable, and have low cross-polarization, making correction relatively straightforward. Such systems easily allow estimation of polarization to an accuracy of the order 1 part in 10000. Beam-induced polarization can be corrected in software – development is under way (ask Preshanth Jagannathan about Full Mueller imaging) Understanding polarization improves calibration and imaging even in the unpolarized case.

entries.description: Requirements for polarization accuracy and system stability

Jones Matrix Algebra

Stokes Visibilities
• Combining the complex fields at antennas1 and 2, the Stokes visibilities can be written as
• The script symbols **I, Q, U, V** remind us that these Stokes Visibilities are complex numbers, related to the (real) source brightness through Fourier transform, e.g.: ℐ 𝑢, 𝑣= ׬ 𝐼𝑒[+2𝜋𝑖𝜐(𝑢𝑙+𝑣𝑚)/𝑐] d𝑙d𝑚 . 𝑙𝑚
• **I** I, **Q** Q, **U** U, **V** V

Measuring Cross-Polarization Terms
- Correction of the X/Y or R/L response for the ‘leakage’ is important, since the D-term amplitude is comparable to the fractional polarization.
- There are two standard ways to proceed (circular base):
   1. Observe a calibrator source of known polarization (preferably zero!) 2. Observe a calibrator of unknown polarization over an extended period.
• **Case 1: Calibrator source known to have zero polarization**
Single observation should suffice to measure leakage terms. Note: In this approximation, only 2Nant-1 terms can be determined. One must be assumed (usually = 0).  All the others are referred to this, thus ‘relative’ D terms.
Measuring Cross-Polarization Terms
**Case 2: Calibrator with significant (or unknown) polarization.**
– You can determine both the (relative) D terms and the calibrator polarizations for an alt-az antenna by observing over a wide range of parallactic angle. (Conway and Kronberg first used this method.)
- As time passes 𝜓𝑝 changes in a known way.
- The source polarization term then rotates w.r.t. the antenna leakage term, allowing a separation.
Source polarization rotates with parallactic angle
Leakage term is fixed

entries.setup: H2O maser feature around S Per

evidence.original_text: Zeeman splitting Process Generates polarized emission Only in spectral lines If magnetic moment: e.g. HI, OH, CN, H2O B-field splits RCP and LCP Separation: 2.8 Hz/mG Polarimetry provides (if detectable) B-field strength at source

entries.context: Zeeman splitting process in spectral lines (e.g., HI, OH, CN, H2O) with magnetic moment

evidence.original_text: Synchrotron Emission Generates polarized emission Main emission mechanism at cm-m wavelength Up to 80% linearly polarized (no circular pol.) < 𝐸source >⊥ 𝐵source

Sadly real sensors are: Imperfectly polarized. Typically, the cross-polarization for circularly polarized systems is ~5% (better with linear). Misaligned with the sky frame. Alt-Az antennas rotate w.r.t. the sky frame as they track a celestial source. The angle describing the misalignment is called the ‘parallactic angle’. Equatorial antennas are fixed w.r.t the sky, but there will be a (small) misalignment of the feed system with the sky. How do these imperfections affect the polarimetry? Start with Antenna rotation (it’s easier)

entries.context: Typical cross-polarization for circularly polarized systems

evidence.original_text: Real VLA S-band D-term amplitudes. Significant frequency structure (2-4 MHz scale). Antenna polarization ~8-10% for this particular VLA antenna w.r.t. the reference antenna.

evidence.original_text: Amplitude |I| is close to a 𝐽0 Bessel function. Zero crossing at 20 k 𝜆 tells that Mars is diameter ~10”. |Q| amplitude ~0 at zero baseline 𝜆 means |Q| zero at 30 k pol. Structures ~8“ scale. VLA, 23 GHz, ‘D’ config. (Jan. 2006)

entries.context: Mars at 23 GHz, VLA 'D' config (Jan. 2006), |Q| zero at 30 kλ

evidence.original_text: Mars emits in the radio as a black body. Shown are false-color coded I,Q,U,P images. V is not shown – all noise – no circular polarization. Resolution is 3.5”, Mars’ diameter is ~10” From the Q and U images alone, we can deduce the polarization is radial, around the limb.

entries.context: Mars I, Q, U, P images from VLA, Mars diameter ~10 arcseconds

evidence.original_text: Polarimetry is a little complicated, but do not be afraid! The polarized state of EM radiation gives valuable insights into the physics of the emission. Well designed systems are stable, and have low cross-polarization, making correction relatively straightforward. Such systems easily allow estimation of polarization to an accuracy of the order 1 part in 10000. Beam-induced polarization can be corrected in software – development . is under way (ask Preshanth Jagannathan about Full Mueller imaging) Understanding polarization improves calibration and imaging even in the unpolarized case.

Sadly real sensors are: Imperfectly polarized. Typically, the cross-polarization for circularly polarized systems is ~5% (better with linear). Misaligned with the sky frame. Alt-Az antennas rotate w.r.t. the sky frame as they track a celestial source. The angle describing the misalignment is called the ‘parallactic angle’. Equatorial antennas are fixed w.r.t the sky, but there will be a (small) misalignment of the feed system with the sky. How do these imperfections affect the polarimetry? Start with Antenna rotation (it’s easier)

There are practical reasons to use linear: Antenna polarizers are natively linear – extra components are needed to produce circular. This hurts performance. These extra components are also generally of narrower bandwidth – it’s harder to build circular systems with really wide bandwidth. At mm wavelengths, the needed phase shifters are not available.

One important practical reason favoring circular: Calibrator sources are often significantly linearly polarized, but have imperceptible circular polarization. Gain calibration is much simpler with circular feeds, especially for ‘snapshot’ style observations.

Polarimetry provides - B-field direction - Turbulence - Indirectly: B-field strength. Zeeman splitting - Generates polarized emission - Only in spectral lines - If magnetic moment: e.g. HI, OH, CN, H2O - B-field splits RCP and LCP - Separation: 2.8 Hz/mG Polarimetry provides (if detectable) B-field strength at source. Scattering/reflection - Modifies polarization state - Thomson scattering: no T dependence Planets / Moon: dielectric transition Polarimetry provides: - Electron densities in cool gas - Dust properties - Lunar dielectric constant. Faraday rotation - Modifies polarization state - Delay between LCP and RCP - Rotates linear pol. Angle - Δ𝜒= 𝜒0 + 𝜙𝜆2 here 𝑛 𝐵∙𝑑𝑙Ԧ 𝑒 𝜙= 0.812 න there Polarimetry provides - Source plasma properties - Intervening plasma properties Graphs of polarization angle against wavelength squared for polarized extragalactic sources in the field (Haverkorn, Katgert, & de Bruyn 2003). - Rare cases: 3D tomography.

Monochromatic radiation does not exist • Narrow slices of frequency width Δ𝜐 , for which fields are described by a 1 single amplitude and phase for a period of t~ Δ𝜐. • Averaging time 𝜏≫Δ𝜐−1 - Note in this case: (100% polarization is not possible) - • Fractional pol.: linear 𝑝= 𝑄2 + 𝑈2 /𝐼≤1 ; circular: 𝑣= 𝑉/𝐼≤1.

Combining the complex fields at antennas1 and 2, the Stokes visibilities can be written as • The script symbols I, Q, U, V remind us that these Stokes Visibilities are complex numbers, related to the (real) source brightness through Fourier transform, e.g.: ℐ 𝑢, 𝑣= ׬ 𝐼𝑒+2𝜋𝑖𝜐(𝑢𝑙+𝑣𝑚)/𝑐 d𝑙d𝑚 . 𝑙𝑚 • I I, Q Q, U U, V V.

One of the ongoing debates is the advantages and disadvantages of Linear and Circular systems, e.g. VLA mostly circular vs. ALMA linear - Point of principle: For full polarization imaging, both systems must provide the same results. Advantages/disadvantages of each are based on points of practicalities. Circular System Linear System - For both, Stokes ‘I’ is the sum of the parallel-hands. - Stokes ‘V’ is the difference of the crossed hand responses for linear (good) and is the difference of the parallel-hand responses for circular (bad) - Stokes ‘Q’ and ‘U’ are differences of cross-hand responses for circular (good), and differences of parallel hands for linear (bad). Both systems provide straightforward derivation of the Stokes’ visibilities from the four correlations. Deriving useable information from differences of large values requires both good stability and good calibration. Hence: - To do good circular polarization using circular system, or good linear polarization with a linear system, we need special care and special methods to ensure good calibration. There are practical reasons to use linear: - Antenna polarizers are natively linear – extra components are needed to produce circular. This hurts performance. - These extra components are also generally of narrower bandwidth – it’s harder to build circular systems with really wide bandwidth. - At mm wavelengths, the needed phase shifters are not available. • One important practical reason favoring circular: - Calibrator sources are often significantly linearly polarized, but have imperceptible circular polarization. - Gain calibration is much simpler with circular feeds, especially for ‘snapshot’ style observations.

Polarimetry is a little complicated, but do not be afraid! - The polarized state of EM radiation gives valuable insights into the physics of the emission. - Well designed systems are stable, and have low cross-polarization, making correction relatively straightforward. - Such systems easily allow estimation of polarization to an accuracy of the order 1 part in 10000. - Beam-induced polarization can be corrected in software – development . - is under way (ask Preshanth Jagannathan about Full Mueller imaging) - Understanding polarization improves calibration and imaging even in the unpolarized case.

Sadly real sensors are: - Imperfectly polarized. Typically, the cross-polarization for circularly polarized systems is ~5% (better with linear). - Misaligned with the sky frame. - Alt-Az antennas rotate w.r.t. the sky frame as they track a celestial source. The angle describing the misalignment is called the ‘parallactic angle’. - Equatorial antennas are fixed w.r.t the sky, but there will be a (small) misalignment of the feed system with the sky. - How do these imperfections affect the polarimetry?

The beam polarization is due to the antenna and feed geometry. Grasp8 calculation by Walter Brisken. (EVLA Memo #58, 2003) Contour intervals: V/I = 4%, Q/I, U/I= 0.2% - Very large V/I polarization is due to the VLA’s offset feeds. - The more modest linear polarization is due to the parabolic antenna. - The beam polarizations can be removed in software – if antenna patterns are known – at considerable computational cost.

Correction of the X/Y or R/L response for the ‘leakage’ is important, since the D-term amplitude is comparable to the fractional polarization. There are two standard ways to proceed (circular base): 1. Observe a calibrator source of known polarization (preferably zero!) 2. Observe a calibrator of unknown polarization over an extended period. • Case 1: Calibrator source known to have zero polarization Single observation should suffice to measure leakage terms. Note: In this approximation, only 2Nant-1 terms can be determined. One must be assumed (usually = 0). All the others are referred to this, thus ‘relative’ D terms.

Beam-induced polarization can be corrected in software – development . - is under way (ask Preshanth Jagannathan about Full Mueller imaging) - Understanding polarization improves calibration and imaging even in the unpolarized case.

Further Reading - K. Rohlfs & T.L. Wilson: Tools of Radio Astronomy (Chapters 2 & 3) - Thompson, Moran & Swenson: Inteferometry and Synthesis in Radio Astronomy - Taylor, Carilli, & Perley: Snythesis Imaging in Radio Astronomy II - Bracewell: The Fourier Transform & Its Applications - Hamaker/Bregman/Sault : Understanding radio polarimetry: papers I -V (1996-2006) - Brentjens & de Bruyn: Faraday rotation measure synthesis (2005) - EVLA Memos by Perley & Sault (#131, #134, #135, #141, #151, #170, #178) - Guide to Observing with the VLA - Polarimetry (https://science.nrao.edu/facilities/vla/docs/manuals/obsguide/modes/pol) - Hales EVLA Memo #201
